content.text: The Robot Operating System 2 (ROS2) is the dominant open-source middleware framework for building modular, distributed robot software. It provides a runtime, communication primitives, standard message types, and a rich ecosystem of pre-built packages — allowing teams to compose complex robot behaviors from isolated, independently-deployed processes.
ROS2 is not an operating system but a
and collection of libraries that runs on top of Linux, macOS, or Windows. An application written with ROS2 is decomposed into independent processes called
(long-running goals with periodic feedback). All three are built on top of the Data Distribution Service (DDS) standard, which handles discovery, serialization, and Quality of Service (QoS) policies.

ROS2 is the successor to ROS1 (released 2007) and was rebuilt from the ground up to address real-time requirements, multi-robot support, security, and production reliability. The first Long-Term Support (LTS) release was ROS2 Foxy (2020); the current LTS as of 2024 is

In the context of agentic AI, ROS2 provides the scaffolding for embodied agents: nodes can wrap LLM inference, perception pipelines, motion planners, and hardware drivers — each independently versioned, restarted, and monitored — with the DDS bus as the message fabric connecting them.

Building robot software without a framework like ROS2 means re-inventing a large class of solved problems. A typical mobile robot application needs: asynchronous communication between a perception pipeline, a planner, and motor controllers running at different rates; hardware abstraction so the same software runs on simulation and the real robot; a way to record, replay, and inspect sensor streams during debugging; and standard message types so a lidar driver from one vendor works with a navigation stack from another.
ROS2 addresses all of these. Its
decouples producers from consumers — a lidar driver node publishes on
regardless of how many downstream nodes subscribe. Its
lets a single file describe an entire multi-node system with parameters, remappings, and lifecycle management. The
tool records and replays every message on the bus, enabling offline debugging and regression testing. And the standard message type library (
) means packages integrate by convention rather than by negotiation.
client libraries (rclpy, rclcpp, rclrust, rclgo) present the same API across Python, C++, Rust, and Go. Nodes in different languages communicate transparently on the same DDS bus.

each topic, service, or action carries Quality-of-Service policies — reliability (reliable vs. best-effort), history depth, deadline, liveliness, lifespan. Fine-grained QoS removes the need to build retry and flow-control logic from scratch.
nodes can run as separate OS processes or be
into a single process (rclcpp component containers / rclpy executors) for lower latency and shared memory.
the managed-node lifecycle (unconfigured → configured → active → finalized) lets an orchestrator bring nodes up and down in a controlled order — essential for hardware initialization sequences.
Gazebo, Isaac Sim, and Webots all expose ROS2 topics and services. The same navigation or agent code runs unchanged on a simulated robot and a physical one.
Nav2 (navigation), MoveIt2 (manipulation), ros2_control (hardware interface), micro-ROS (microcontrollers), and hundreds of community packages are all production-ready and maintained.
RViz2 for 3D visualization, rqt for GUI introspection,
CLI for live debugging, and rosbag2 for recording/replay.
DDS, QoS policies, lifecycle management, ament build system, colcon, and launch files all have their own concepts. Productivity ramp-up for a new team is measured in weeks.

each message is serialized to CDR (DDS wire format) and deserialized on the receiving node. For high-bandwidth sensor streams (dense pointclouds, HD cameras), shared memory transports (iceoryx / rmw_cyclonedds) are required to avoid CPU saturation.
the ament_cmake / ament_python build system and colcon tool add ceremony for packaging. Custom message types require code generation and recompilation.
multicast-based peer discovery defaults can cause problems on WiFi, VLANs, and Docker/Kubernetes networks. Manual
or DDS peer lists are often needed.
rclpy nodes run Python, which means the GIL constrains true parallelism within a single process. Tight real-time loops (< 1 ms cycle) require C++ (rclcpp) or a separate hardware-interface node.
rclpy does not support real-time OS (RT Linux) scheduling by default. Hard real-time guarantees require rclcpp with RT thread priorities and lock-free executors.
(C++) with a unique name and optional namespace. Nodes are discovered automatically by the DDS participant discovery protocol (RTPS).
are named data streams identified by a string (e.g.

; any number of subscribers receive the message. The connection is formed automatically when publisher and subscriber use the same topic name and message type and their QoS policies are compatible. Topics are the backbone of ROS2: sensor data, odometry, and velocity commands are all topics.
implement remote procedure call. A service server registers a callback that receives a

and awaits the response future. Services are synchronous at the application level — the caller blocks (or awaits) until the server responds. Use services for operations with a well-defined completion boundary and short duration: querying the current map, resetting a sensor, fetching a path plan.
are built on top of services and topics and support long-running goals. An action client sends a
; the action server accepts it and begins executing, publishing periodic
messages as it progresses, and eventually sending a
. The client can cancel the goal at any time. Navigation, arm manipulation, and multi-step agent tasks are all natural fits for actions.

is per-node: each node declares parameters (with types and default values) and exposes them to the ROS2 CLI and launch system. Parameters change node behavior at runtime without recompilation: PID gains, topic names, feature flags. Parameters are declared with

library manages coordinate frame transforms. Every sensor, actuator, and robot link has a frame ID; tf2 maintains the transform tree and answers queries like "what is the position of the robot's hand in the world frame at time T?" using buffered transform messages published on the

The diagram below shows a representative ROS2 node graph for a mobile robot agent. Nodes communicate via topics (solid arrows), services (dashed amber), and actions (dashed purple). All traffic passes through the DDS middleware layer, which handles discovery and QoS.

attrs.alt: ROS2 architecture: Robot Agent Node communicating with Nav2 Controller and Sensor/Hardware nodes via Topics, Services, and Actions over DDS middleware

content.text: ROS2 is appropriate when you are building software for a physical or simulated robot, need to integrate hardware sensors and actuators, or need to reuse components from the robotics ecosystem (Nav2, MoveIt2, ros2_control). It is also the right choice for multi-node agentic systems where nodes run at different rates (a 10 Hz perception loop and a 200 Hz controller cannot share a process comfortably) or need independent restart policies.

Use ROS2 when: the system has physical hardware (sensors, actuators, robot arms); the team needs to reuse existing robotics stacks; simulation/hardware parity is important; or the system has strict component isolation requirements (crash in a sensor driver should not crash the planner).

the right choice for: pure software agents with no hardware integration; systems where all logic fits in a single process and a message bus adds unnecessary indirection; or cloud-only deployments where DDS discovery, UDP multicast, and port management conflict with existing network policies. For those use cases, direct async frameworks (asyncio, Temporal) or multi-agent orchestration tools are a better fit.

# Ubuntu 22.04 + ROS2 Humble (LTS through 2027)
sudo apt install software-properties-common
sudo add-apt-repository universe
sudo curl -sSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/ros/rosdistro/master/ros.key \
    -o /usr/share/keyrings/ros-archive-keyring.gpg
echo "deb [arch=$(dpkg --print-architecture) signed-by=/usr/share/keyrings/ros-archive-keyring.gpg] \
    http://packages.ros.org/ros2/ubuntu $(. /etc/os-release && echo $UBUNTU_CODENAME) main" \
    | sudo tee /etc/apt/sources.list.d/ros2.list
sudo apt update
sudo apt install ros-humble-desktop         # full install: rviz2, rqt, examples
sudo apt install python3-colcon-common-extensions  # build tool

# Source the install in every shell (add to ~/.bashrc)
source /opt/ros/humble/setup.bash

# Create workspace
mkdir -p ~/ros2_ws/src && cd ~/ros2_ws

# Create a Python package
cd src
ros2 pkg create --build-type ament_python my_agent_pkg \
    --dependencies rclpy std_msgs sensor_msgs geometry_msgs

# Build
cd ~/ros2_ws
colcon build --symlink-install   # --symlink-install avoids rebuild on .py edits
source install/setup.bash

# Run a node
ros2 run my_agent_pkg my_node

# Inspect the live graph
ros2 node list
ros2 topic list
ros2 topic echo /scan             # print messages live
ros2 topic hz /scan               # measure publish rate
ros2 node info /my_agent_node     # subscriptions, publications, services
The following publisher node publishes a
at 1 Hz, and the subscriber node receives and logs it. This is the minimal unit of ROS2 communication.

# my_agent_pkg/my_agent_pkg/status_publisher.py
import rclpy
from rclpy.node import Node
from std_msgs.msg import String

class StatusPublisher(Node):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__('status_publisher')
        self.pub = self.create_publisher(String, '/agent_status', 10)  # queue depth 10
        self.timer = self.create_timer(1.0, self.publish_status)  # 1 Hz
        self.step = 0

    def publish_status(self):
        msg = String()
        msg.data = f'Agent alive — step {self.step}'
        self.pub.publish(msg)
        self.get_logger().info(f'Published: {msg.data}')
        self.step += 1

def main():
    rclpy.init()
    node = StatusPublisher()
    rclpy.spin(node)       # blocks, processes callbacks in the executor
    rclpy.shutdown()

if __name__ == '__main__':
    main()

# my_agent_pkg/my_agent_pkg/status_subscriber.py
import rclpy
from rclpy.node import Node
from std_msgs.msg import String

class StatusSubscriber(Node):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__('status_subscriber')
        self.sub = self.create_subscription(
            String,
            '/agent_status',
            self.on_status,
            10,   # QoS: keep last 10
        )

    def on_status(self, msg: String):
        self.get_logger().info(f'Received: {msg.data}')

def main():
    rclpy.init()
    node = StatusSubscriber()
    rclpy.spin(node)
    rclpy.shutdown()

if __name__ == '__main__':
    main()

Actions are used for long-running tasks. The following example shows an action server that simulates a navigation-to-pose goal, and a client that sends a goal, monitors feedback, and retrieves the result.

# action server
import rclpy
from rclpy.node import Node
from rclpy.action import ActionServer
from nav2_msgs.action import NavigateToPose
import time

class FakeNavServer(Node):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__('fake_nav_server')
        self._server = ActionServer(
            self,
            NavigateToPose,
            'navigate_to_pose',
            self.execute_callback,
        )

    def execute_callback(self, goal_handle):
        self.get_logger().info('Received navigation goal')
        goal = goal_handle.request.pose

        feedback_msg = NavigateToPose.Feedback()
        for i in range(1, 6):
            if goal_handle.is_cancel_requested:
                goal_handle.canceled()
                return NavigateToPose.Result()

            feedback_msg.distance_remaining = float(5 - i)
            goal_handle.publish_feedback(feedback_msg)
            self.get_logger().info(f'Distance remaining: {feedback_msg.distance_remaining:.1f} m')
            time.sleep(1.0)

        goal_handle.succeed()
        result = NavigateToPose.Result()
        return result

def main():
    rclpy.init()
    rclpy.spin(FakeNavServer())
    rclpy.shutdown()

# action client
import rclpy
from rclpy.node import Node
from rclpy.action import ActionClient
from nav2_msgs.action import NavigateToPose
from geometry_msgs.msg import PoseStamped

class NavClient(Node):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__('nav_client')
        self._client = ActionClient(self, NavigateToPose, 'navigate_to_pose')

    def send_goal(self, x: float, y: float):
        self._client.wait_for_server()

        goal = NavigateToPose.Goal()
        goal.pose = PoseStamped()
        goal.pose.header.frame_id = 'map'
        goal.pose.pose.position.x = x
        goal.pose.pose.position.y = y
        goal.pose.pose.orientation.w = 1.0

        future = self._client.send_goal_async(
            goal,
            feedback_callback=self.feedback_cb,
        )
        future.add_done_callback(self.goal_response_cb)

    def feedback_cb(self, feedback_msg):
        fb = feedback_msg.feedback
        self.get_logger().info(f'Distance remaining: {fb.distance_remaining:.1f} m')

    def goal_response_cb(self, future):
        handle = future.result()
        if not handle.accepted:
            self.get_logger().error('Goal rejected')
            return
        result_future = handle.get_result_async()
        result_future.add_done_callback(self.result_cb)

    def result_cb(self, future):
        self.get_logger().info('Navigation complete')

def main():
    rclpy.init()
    node = NavClient()
    node.send_goal(3.0, 1.5)
    rclpy.spin(node)
    rclpy.shutdown()
Consider a warehouse picking robot with an LLM-based task planner. The system runs on a Jetson AGX Orin with ROS2 Humble. Five nodes are always active:
(the full Nav2 stack — map server, AMCL, controller, planner),

topic (a custom message with a natural language task string). On receipt, it calls the LLM to decompose the task into a sequence of navigation goals and arm commands. Each navigation goal is sent as an action goal to Nav2's
action server. The node awaits feedback, handles preemption (e.g., if an obstacle blocks the planned path), and logs task progress.

The entire system is launched with a single launch file. Lifecycle management ensures that the hardware interface node is activated before the Nav2 stack, and the agent planner only activates after Nav2 reports ready. A rosbag2 recording runs in the background capturing all sensor topics for post-mission analysis.

# agent_planner_node.py  — LLM-based task orchestrator
import rclpy
from rclpy.node import Node
from rclpy.action import ActionClient
from std_msgs.msg import String
from nav2_msgs.action import NavigateToPose
from geometry_msgs.msg import PoseStamped
import anthropic, json

class AgentPlannerNode(Node):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__('agent_planner')
        self._nav_client = ActionClient(self, NavigateToPose, 'navigate_to_pose')
        self._task_sub = self.create_subscription(
            String, '/task_request', self.on_task, 10
        )
        self._llm = anthropic.Anthropic()
        self.get_logger().info('Agent planner ready')

    def on_task(self, msg: String):
        self.get_logger().info(f'Task received: {msg.data}')
        goals = self._plan_task(msg.data)
        for goal in goals:
            self._navigate_to(goal['x'], goal['y'])

    def _plan_task(self, task_text: str) -> list[dict]:
        """Call the LLM to convert a task description into a list of (x, y) waypoints."""
        response = self._llm.messages.create(
            model='claude-opus-4-5',
            max_tokens=512,
            messages=[{
                'role': 'user',
                'content': (
                    f'Given the warehouse map with shelves at A1(1,2), A2(1,4), B1(3,2), B2(3,4) '
                    f'and the dock at (0,0), convert this task into a JSON list of {{x,y}} waypoints: {task_text}'
                )
            }],
        )
        try:
            return json.loads(response.content[0].text)
        except Exception:
            self.get_logger().error('LLM returned unparseable plan')
            return []

    def _navigate_to(self, x: float, y: float):
        self._nav_client.wait_for_server(timeout_sec=5.0)
        goal = NavigateToPose.Goal()
        goal.pose = PoseStamped()
        goal.pose.header.frame_id = 'map'
        goal.pose.pose.position.x = x
        goal.pose.pose.position.y = y
        goal.pose.pose.orientation.w = 1.0
        # send_goal_async returns immediately; we spin on feedback via callback
        self._nav_client.send_goal_async(goal, feedback_callback=self._feedback_cb)
        self.get_logger().info(f'Navigation goal sent: ({x}, {y})')

    def _feedback_cb(self, feedback_msg):
        d = feedback_msg.feedback.distance_remaining
        self.get_logger().info(f'Distance remaining: {d:.2f} m')

def main():
    rclpy.init()
    rclpy.spin(AgentPlannerNode())
    rclpy.shutdown()
Calling rclpy.spin() on a node that has blocking I/O in a callback:
is single-threaded by default. A blocking LLM call inside a subscription callback blocks all other callbacks on the node. Use a
for callbacks that block, or offload the blocking work to a thread pool.
Using incompatible QoS between publisher and subscriber:
will not connect — no warning is raised, messages simply do not flow. Always check QoS compatibility. Run
Forgetting to source the install directory:
in every new terminal. Without this,
Domain ID conflicts in shared environments:
ROS2 uses DDS domain IDs (0–232) for isolation. On a shared network with multiple robots, set
to a unique integer per robot. Default (0) causes all robots on the network to see each other's messages.
Publishing large messages synchronously in a tight loop:
publishing a dense pointcloud (3 MB) at 20 Hz inside a timer callback runs fine on localhost but causes DDS queue overflows and lost messages on a wireless link. Tune
QoS, or use compressed image topics.
Using global time (time.time()) instead of ROS time:
in simulation with simulated time enabled (
returns wall-clock time, not simulation time. Use
) so that multiple robots can run on the same ROS2 domain without collisions. The launch system's
and overridable from the launch file without source changes.
Use lifecycle nodes for hardware drivers:
hooks gives the orchestrator control over initialization order and graceful shutdown.
Set appropriate QoS for each topic:
(best-effort, depth 5) for high-rate sensor streams where occasional loss is acceptable. Use
with a history depth ≥ 1 for command and configuration topics where every message must be delivered.
Prefer composition over process isolation for latency-sensitive paths:

co-locating nodes in a component container (rclcpp) or using intra-process communication in rclpy avoids DDS serialization overhead on the hot path. Reserve separate processes for fault isolation of hardware drivers.
or select specific topics during every test run. Bags are the primary debugging tool: replay them offline to reproduce issues without needing the physical robot.
ROS2's throughput is bounded by the DDS implementation's serialization overhead and the executor's callback scheduling. In typical benchmarks on a Jetson Orin:
round-trip latency < 0.2 ms on localhost with Cyclone DDS; throughput >> 10,000 msg/s.

loopback throughput limited to ~10–15 Hz at full resolution without shared memory. With iceoryx shared-memory transport (rmw_iceoryx), the copy is eliminated and throughput scales to the camera frame rate.

DDS handles one-to-many efficiently; adding 10 subscribers to a topic adds negligible overhead on the publisher side in reliable mode. Best-effort topics add zero overhead regardless of subscriber count.
polls at ~1 kHz. For nodes with many callbacks,
matching the number of independent callback groups reduces latency.
For LLM-based agent nodes, the bottleneck is the inference call, not ROS2. The ROS2 overhead is negligible compared to a 200–2000 ms LLM round trip. Use async service clients (
) so the node executor continues processing other callbacks while waiting for the LLM response.

3D visualization tool. Displays sensor data (pointclouds, images, laser scans), robot model (URDF), navigation costmaps, and tf frames live as the robot operates. Indispensable for debugging perception and navigation issues.
Qt-based plugin framework with graph introspection (rqt_graph), topic monitors (rqt_topic), parameter editors (rqt_reconfigure), and bag viewers.
records and replays bag files in SQLite or MCAP format. Run
during a test drive and replay with

the full navigation stack for ROS2 — map server, AMCL localization, global planner (NavFn, Smac), local controller (DWB, Regulated Pure Pursuit), costmap 2D, and recovery behaviors. Supports GPS-based navigation and multi-floor maps.
the arm manipulation planning stack. Provides inverse kinematics, motion planning (OMPL, Pilz), collision checking, and grasp planning. Integrates with Gazebo and Isaac Sim via ros2_control.
hardware abstraction layer for robot actuators. Presents a unified
to controllers (joint trajectory, diff drive, gripper) regardless of the underlying hardware.
brings ROS2 to microcontrollers (STM32, ESP32, RP2040) over serial or UDP. A micro-ROS agent on the host computer bridges the microcontroller's nodes into the ROS2 graph.
the standard simulator for ROS2. Exposes robot sensors as ROS2 topics via a bridge plugin, enabling full hardware-in-the-loop simulation.
Soft RT (C++), no hard RT in Python
Growing; bridges into ROS2 via zenoh-bridge-ros2dds
Multi-robot / edge-cloud pipelines, ROS2 WAN bridging
Pure software agents, no hardware integration
(Secure ROS2), a toolchain that applies DDS Security:
(TLS over DDS). By default, ROS2 runs without encryption — any node on the same DDS domain can publish to any topic, including
and lifecycle transitions. In production deployments, always enable SROS2 for safety-critical actuator topics.
in environments where the robot should not receive ROS2 messages from external hosts, or configure
to isolate different robots. When running on Kubernetes or Docker, use
(required for DDS multicast discovery) or switch to Zenoh as the RMW layer, which supports bridged NAT-friendly connectivity.
with a lifecycle manager that monitors node health and restarts failed nodes. The
provided by Nav2 demonstrates this pattern: it activates nodes in dependency order and restarts the entire stack if any critical node fails its heartbeat.
Enable SROS2 for any actuator topic:

values from a subscribed topic without range checks. A node relaying LLM-generated velocity commands should clamp linear velocity to the robot's safe range before forwarding to the hardware interface.
hardware interface nodes should implement a watchdog: if no
message arrives within N milliseconds (e.g., 500 ms), command zero velocity. This stops the robot if the planner crashes or the network drops.

Think of a ROS2 robot system as a newsroom. Each journalist (node) works independently, publishing articles on bulletin boards (topics) for anyone who cares to read. If a journalist needs an immediate answer, they pick up the phone (service). If they need to assign a long investigative piece with status updates, they file a formal assignment (action) that the editor accepts, provides interim reports on, and eventually closes with a published article or a rejection.

The DDS layer is the building's internal communications infrastructure — it routes messages, ensures reliable delivery based on the channel's policy, and discovers new journalists automatically when they join the newsroom. The parameter server is each journalist's personal notepad — preferences and settings visible to the editor (launch system) but owned by the journalist (node).
ROS2 decomposes robot software into independent
(long-running goals with feedback) — all built on the DDS standard.
QoS policies (reliability, history depth, deadline, liveliness) are configured per-topic and must match between publisher and subscriber; mismatched QoS silently prevents topic connections.

The standard ecosystem — Nav2 for navigation, MoveIt2 for manipulation, ros2_control for hardware, rosbag2 for recording — means most robotics capabilities are available as drop-in packages rather than custom code.
Avoid blocking calls inside subscription callbacks when using the default
for nodes that call LLMs or perform I/O.
per robot on shared networks, enable SROS2 for actuator topics in production, and implement watchdog timers in hardware interface nodes to fail safe on planner or network loss.

For large sensor streams (pointclouds, HD cameras), shared-memory transports (iceoryx, Zenoh) eliminate DDS copy overhead; for LLM-based agent nodes, the inference latency dominates and ROS2 overhead is negligible.
ROS2 Humble is the recommended LTS release (supported through May 2027); always source
